_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: He was named for a liberator. He is now a warning.

museum_objects.title: 1879 Silver Bolívar (Brussels Mint)
museum_objects.description: The first bolívar coin, struck in Brussels and named after independence leader Simón Bolívar.
museum_objects.significance: Named for the man who freed a continent, Simón's coin started as a tribute — before oil, debt, and inflation turned that name into something closer to a cautionary tale.
museum_objects.museum: Banco Central de Venezuela Numismatic Collection
museum_objects.plain_language: The very first bolívar coin, made in Belgium in 1879 and named after Simón Bolívar, the hero who helped free much of South America from Spain.
museum_objects.institution: Banco Central de Venezuela Numismatic Collection

museum_objects.title: Battle of Carabobo Painting
museum_objects.description: A painting by Arturo Michelena depicting the decisive 1821 battle that secured Venezuelan independence.
museum_objects.significance: This is the founding myth Simón was born to carry — a currency named after the war that made an independent Venezuela possible in the first place.
museum_objects.plain_language: A painting of the famous battle that won Venezuela its independence — the historical event Simón's currency is named to honor.

museum_objects.title: Zumaque-I Oil Well Historical Site
museum_objects.description: The site of Venezuela's first major oil discovery, which transformed the national economy.
museum_objects.significance: The exact moment Simón stopped being just about independence and started being about oil — a discovery that would define, and eventually destabilize, an entire nation's currency.
museum_objects.plain_language: The site of Venezuela's first big oil discovery in 1914, the moment oil started shaping the entire country's money.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: 1976 Oil Nationalization Archive
museum_objects.description: New York Times archival coverage of Venezuela's formal nationalization of its oil industry.
museum_objects.significance: The day Venezuela decided oil belonged entirely to the state — a decision that would tie Simón's fate to the price of crude for the next half-century.
museum_objects.plain_language: An old New York Times article covering the day Venezuela took full government control of its oil industry.

museum_objects.title: BCV 50th Anniversary Museum Publication
museum_objects.description: An official publication by Venezuela's central bank documenting fifty years of monetary history.
museum_objects.significance: Even the bank tasked with managing Simón's fate has had to publicly reckon with just how far he's fallen from his original promise.
museum_objects.plain_language: An official book from Venezuela's central bank looking back at fifty years of its currency's history.

world_map.map_description: Simón's bolívar was born from independence-era pride, transformed by the discovery of oil, and has suffered some of the worst hyperinflation crises in modern history.

nodes.label: Caracas (Plaza Bolívar)
nodes.description: Venezuela's capital, named central square honoring the independence leader the bolívar is named for.

nodes.label: BCV, Maracaibo
nodes.description: Major economic hub near Venezuela's oil-producing region, historically vital to the bolívar's value.

nodes.label: Zumaque-I Well, Mene Grande
nodes.description: Site of Venezuela's first major oil discovery in 1914, which transformed the national economy and currency's fate.

nodes.label: Angel Falls
nodes.description: A natural wonder symbolizing Venezuela's vast, underutilized potential beyond its oil dependency.
